title: Coconut Chutney

overview.description: A fresh South Indian chutney made from coconut, roasted chana dal, green chilli and ginger, finished with a fragrant tempering of mustard seeds and curry leaves.

ingredients: 1 tsp neutral oil or coconut oil
method: Add the grated coconut, roasted chana dal, green chillies, ginger, lemon juice, salt and approximately 100ml water to a blender.
Blend until smooth, adding more water as necessary to reach a thick but spoonable consistency.
Taste and adjust the salt, chilli and lemon juice.
Transfer the chutney to a serving bowl.
Heat the oil in a small pan over a medium heat.
Add the mustard seeds and cook until they begin to pop.
Add the curry leaves and dried red chilli if using, taking care as they may splutter.
Add the asafoetida if using and cook for a few seconds.
Pour the tempering over the chutney.
Stir gently or leave the tempering on top to serve.

learning.did-you-know: Coconut chutney is served throughout South India, and small changes in ingredients and texture are common between regions and households.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to balance a fresh chutney and use tempering to add aromatic flavour and texture at the end.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A versatile South Indian accompaniment that teaches how a few fresh ingredients can create a bright, creamy dip and how a tempering can transform its flavour.
chapman-tips: Frozen grated coconut works very well and is often more practical than finding a fresh coconut.
Add the water gradually; a chutney that is too thin is difficult to correct.
The roasted chana dal adds body and a subtle nutty flavour.
The tempering should be hot enough to release the aromas but not so hot that the mustard seeds and curry leaves burn.
Taste the chutney after it has rested for a few minutes; the flavours become more integrated.
For a smoother chutney, blend for longer and add a little more water.
notes.batch-cooking: Best made fresh, but it can be refrigerated for a day or two. Freezing is possible but the texture may become slightly less smooth after thawing.
notes.leftovers: Use as a dip for vegetables, spread into sandwiches or wraps, or serve alongside rice dishes.
